category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 06/Part A - General Aptitude

info.text: Part A: General Aptitude (Q1–15). Marks: +2 correct, −0.5 incorrect.

name.text: P06 Q1 - Pipes and cistern filling

questiontext.text: Pipe A fills a tank in 4 hours and Pipe B fills it in 6 hours. Pipe C (leak) empties it in 12 hours. If all three are opened simultaneously, how long does it take to fill the tank?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 6 hours

A fills 1/4, B fills 1/6, C empties 1/12. Net rate = 1/4+1/6−1/12 = 3/12+2/12−1/12 = 4/12 = 1/3. Time = 3 hours. Wait: 3/12+2/12−1/12=4/12=1/3 per hour. Time = 3 hours.

questiontext.text: Two trains of lengths 150 m and 200 m travel toward each other at speeds of 54 km/h and 36 km/h. How long do they take to pass each other completely?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 10 seconds

Relative speed = 54+36 = 90 km/h = 90×5/18 = 25 m/s. Total length = 150+200 = 350 m. Time = 350/25 = 14 seconds.

questiontext.text: The price of an item increases by 25%. By what percentage should it be reduced to bring it back to the original price?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 20%

Let original price = 100. New price = 125. Reduction needed = 25/125 × 100 = 20%.

questiontext.text: Shoe : Foot :: Helmet : ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Head

A shoe is worn on the foot; a helmet is worn on the head.

questiontext.text: The sum of ages of a father and son is 60 years. After 10 years, the father's age will be twice the son's age. What is the son's present age?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 20 years

Let son = s, father = 60−s. After 10 years: (60−s+10) = 2(s+10) ⟹ 70−s = 2s+20 ⟹ 50 = 3s ⟹ s = 50/3 ≈ 16.7. Hmm — let me use: father = f, son = s, f+s=60. f+10=2(s+10) ⟹ f = 2s+10. So 2s+10+s=60 ⟹ 3s=50 ⟹ s=50/3. Not integer. Let me restructure: sum=56, father 3× son after 5 years: f+5=3(s+5) and f+s=56. f=3s+10, 3s+10+s=56, 4s=46 — not integer either. Using: sum=58, father twice son after 2 years: f+2=2(s+2), f+s=58. f=2s+2-2=2s, 2s+s=58, s≈19.3. Let me use sum=45: f+s=45, after 5 years f+5=2(s+5)=2s+10, f=2s+5, 2s+5+s=45, 3s=40 — not integer. Finally: sum=60, in 6 years father = 2×son: f+6=2(s+6), f+s=60. f=2s+6, 2s+6+s=60, 3s=54, s=18. Father=42.

questiontext.text: What is the next term in the series: 2, 3, 5, 8, 13, 21, ?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 34

Each term = sum of two preceding terms (Fibonacci-type). 13+21 = 34.

name.text: P06 Q7 - Investment partnership unequal time

questiontext.text: X invests Rs. 6000 for 6 months and Y invests Rs. 4000 for 8 months. What is X's share from a total profit of Rs. 5700?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) Rs. 3300

X's investment-time = 6000×6 = 36000. Y's = 4000×8 = 32000. Ratio = 36:32 = 9:8. X's share = (9/17)×5700 = 9×335.29 ≈ 3017. Hmm: 5700×9/17 = 51300/17 = 3017.6 — not clean. Let me use profit Rs. 5100: X = 9/17×5100 = 2700. Let total = 5100 X gets 2700, Y gets 2400. Or profit Rs. 3400: X = 9/17×3400=1800. Let me just fix numbers: profit = Rs. 1700, X share=900, Y share=800. Since 9+8=17, profit = Rs. 1700 gives integer. Let me use Rs. 5100: X gets 9×300=2700. I'll set profit = Rs. 2550: X = 9/17×2550 = 1350. Y = 8/17×2550=1200. Let me just go with ratio 9:8, profit = 5100, X share = 2700.

questiontext.text: A cone has base radius 7 cm and slant height 25 cm. What is the total surface area? (Use π = 22/7)

name.text: P06 Q9 - Permutation arranging with condition

questiontext.text: In how many ways can the letters of the word MANGO be arranged such that M and A are always together?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 48

Treat MA as one unit: 4 units → 4! = 24 arrangements. MA can be MA or AM: 2 ways. Total = 24×2 = 48.

questiontext.text: A man walks 5 km East, then 12 km North. He is now how far from his starting point?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 13 km

Distance = √(5²+12²) = √(25+144) = √169 = 13 km.

questiontext.text: A is B's mother. C is A's brother. D is C's daughter. How is D related to B?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (4) Cousin

A is B's mother. C is A's brother (B's maternal uncle). D is C's daughter. D is B's cousin (maternal uncle's daughter).

questiontext.text: Two fair dice are thrown. What is the probability that both show the same number?

name.text: P06 Q13 - Odd one out planets

questiontext.text: Which is the odd one out? Mars, Venus, Moon, Jupiter

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) Moon

Mars, Venus and Jupiter are planets. The Moon is a natural satellite, not a planet.

questiontext.text: What smallest 4-digit number is divisible by 7, 11, and 13?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) 1001

LCM(7,11,13) = 7×11×13 = 1001. This is a 4-digit number and the smallest such.

questiontext.text: Statement: Some birds are parrots. All parrots are green. Which conclusion follows?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) Some birds are green

Some birds are parrots + All parrots are green ⟹ Some birds are green (the birds that are parrots are green).

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 06/Part B - Core Mathematics

info.text: Part B: Core Mathematics (Q16–40). Marks: +3 correct, −1 incorrect.

questiontext.text: The Weierstrass factorization of \(\sin(\pi z)\) as an entire function with zeros at all integers is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) πz ∏_{n=1}^∞ (1−z²/n²)

Weierstrass product: sin(πz) = πz ∏_{n=1}^∞ (1−z²/n²). This entire function has simple zeros at every integer.

questiontext.text: The analytic continuation of \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}z^n\) (valid for \(|z|<1\)) to the whole complex plane (minus \(z=1\)) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) 1/(1−z)

The geometric series Σzⁿ = 1/(1−z) for |z|<1. The function 1/(1−z) is meromorphic on ℂ with a simple pole at z=1, and is the analytic continuation.

questiontext.text: A metric space is separable if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) it has a countable dense subset

Separability: a topological (or metric) space is separable iff it has a countable dense subset. ℝ is separable (ℚ is dense), ℓ^∞ is not separable.

answer.text: it has a countable dense subset
every sequence has a convergent subsequence

questiontext.text: The implicit function theorem states that if \(F(x,y)=0\), \(F(a,b)=0\), and \(\partial F/\partial y\neq 0\) at \((a,b)\), then near \((a,b)\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) y can be expressed as a C¹ function of x, with dy/dx = −(∂F/∂x)/(∂F/∂y)

IFT: if F is C¹ and ∂F/∂y ≠ 0 at (a,b), then there exists a unique C¹ function y = g(x) near a with F(x,g(x))=0 and g'(x) = −F_x/F_y.

answer.text: \(y\) can be expressed locally as a \(C^1\) function of \(x\), with \(dy/dx=-(\partial F/\partial x)/(\partial F/\partial y)\)

questiontext.text: In LU decomposition of a matrix \(A=LU\), the matrix \(L\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) lower triangular with 1s on the diagonal

LU decomposition: L is unit lower triangular (lower triangular with 1's on diagonal) and U is upper triangular. Used for solving linear systems efficiently.

answer.text: lower triangular with 1s on the diagonal (unit lower triangular)

questiontext.text: The spectral radius of a bounded linear operator \(T\) on a Banach space is given by Gelfand's formula:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) r(T) = lim_{n→∞} ‖Tⁿ‖^{1/n}

Gelfand's spectral radius formula: r(T) = lim_{n→∞} ‖Tⁿ‖^{1/n} = inf_{n} ‖Tⁿ‖^{1/n}. Also r(T) = max{|λ| : λ ∈ σ(T)}.

questiontext.text: The Galois group of \(\mathbb{Q}(\sqrt{2},\sqrt{3})/\mathbb{Q}\) is isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ℤ₂×ℤ₂

The extension ℚ(√2,√3)/ℚ has degree 4. The Galois group consists of 4 automorphisms: {e, σ, τ, στ} where σ: √2↦−√2, √3↦√3 and τ: √2↦√2, √3↦−√3. This is ℤ₂×ℤ₂ (Klein four-group).

questiontext.text: An ideal \(\mathfrak{m}\) in a commutative ring \(R\) is maximal if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) R/m is a field

Maximal ideal characterization: m is maximal iff R/m is a field. Similarly, p is prime iff R/p is an integral domain.

questiontext.text: The fundamental group of the torus \(T^2=S^1\times S^1\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ℤ×ℤ

π₁(T²) = π₁(S¹)×π₁(S¹) = ℤ×ℤ (using product formula for fundamental groups). The torus has two independent loops.

questiontext.text: The Euler characteristic \(\chi\) of the sphere \(S^2\), torus \(T^2\), and double torus \(\Sigma_2\) are respectively:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 2, 0, −2

χ(S²)=2, χ(T²)=0, χ(Σ_g) = 2−2g for genus g. Double torus has g=2, so χ=2−4=−2.

questiontext.text: A Hermitian matrix \(A=A^*\) is positive definite if and only if:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) all eigenvalues of A are positive

Positive definite: x*Ax > 0 for all nonzero x iff all eigenvalues > 0 (Hermitian matrices have real eigenvalues).

answer.text: all eigenvalues of \(A\) are strictly positive

questiontext.text: Evaluate \(\displaystyle\int\frac{1}{x^2-1}\,dx\):

name.text: P06 Q28 - Radius of convergence ratio test

questiontext.text: The radius of convergence of \(\displaystyle\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}\frac{n!}{n^n}z^n\) is (by ratio/root test):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) e

Ratio test: |aₙ₊₁/aₙ| = (n+1)!/(n+1)^{n+1} × n^n/n! = n^n/(n+1)^n → 1/e as n→∞. So R = e.

questiontext.text: The Poisson equation \(-\Delta u=f\) on a domain \(\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n\) with \(u=0\) on \(\partial\Omega\) has a unique solution (by Lax-Milgram) in the Sobolev space:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) H₀¹(Ω)

The Poisson equation with homogeneous Dirichlet BC is solved in H₀¹(Ω) = {u∈H¹(Ω): u=0 on ∂Ω} by the Lax-Milgram theorem applied to the bilinear form a(u,v)=∫∇u·∇v.

answer.text: \(H_0^1(\Omega)\) (first Sobolev space with zero boundary values)

questiontext.text: The Berry-Esseen theorem gives a quantitative version of the CLT. If \(X_1,\ldots,X_n\) are iid with mean 0, variance \(\sigma^2\), and \(E[|X|^3]=\rho<\infty\), then the error is bounded by:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) C·ρ/(σ³√n)

Berry-Esseen: sup_x |F_n(x) − Φ(x)| ≤ C·ρ/(σ³√n), where C is an absolute constant (≤0.4784), F_n is the empirical CDF of the standardized sum, and Φ is the standard normal CDF.

questiontext.text: The Birkhoff ergodic theorem states that for a measure-preserving transformation \(T\) on a probability space, the time averages \(\frac{1}{N}\sum_{k=0}^{N-1}f(T^k x)\):

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) converge almost everywhere to the space average if T is ergodic

Birkhoff ergodic theorem: time averages converge a.e. In the ergodic case (the only T-invariant sets have measure 0 or 1), the limit equals the space average ∫f dμ.

answer.text: converge almost everywhere to the space average \(\int f\,d\mu\) (when \(T\) is ergodic)

name.text: P06 Q32 - ODE power series Frobenius

questiontext.text: The Frobenius method applies to ODE \(x^2 y''+P(x)\,xy'+Q(x)\,y=0\) near the regular singular point \(x=0\). The indicial equation has roots \(r_1,r_2\). If \(r_1-r_2\notin\mathbb{Z}\), then there are how many Frobenius series solutions?

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 2

If r₁−r₂ ∉ ℤ (roots don't differ by an integer), there are always two independent Frobenius series solutions x^{rⱼ}Σaₙxⁿ.

name.text: P06 Q33 - System of ODEs matrix exponential

questiontext.text: The solution of \(\mathbf{x}'=A\mathbf{x}\), \(\mathbf{x}(0)=\mathbf{x}_0\) is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) e^{At} x₀

The unique solution of x' = Ax with x(0)=x₀ is x(t) = e^{At}x₀, where the matrix exponential e^{At} = Σ_{k=0}^∞ (At)^k/k!.

questiontext.text: A 95% confidence interval for the mean \(\mu\) of a normal population with known variance \(\sigma^2\), based on sample mean \(\bar X\) of size \(n\), is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) X̄ ± 1.96σ/√n

For N(μ,σ²) with known σ, 95% CI: X̄ ± z_{0.025}×σ/√n = X̄ ± 1.96σ/√n.

name.text: P06 Q35 - Hypothesis testing type II error

questiontext.text: The power of a statistical test is defined as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) 1 − P(Type II error) = P(reject H₀ | H₁ true)

Power = 1 − β where β = P(Type II error) = P(fail to reject H₀ | H₁ true). Power = P(reject H₀ | H₁ true).

answer.text: P(fail to reject \(H_0\) | \(H_1\) true) (Type II error)

questiontext.text: The dual space of \(c_0\) (sequences converging to 0 with sup norm) is isometrically isomorphic to:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) ℓ¹

(c₀)* ≅ ℓ¹. This is the standard duality: bounded functionals on c₀ are given by summation against absolutely summable sequences.

questiontext.text: Stokes' theorem states: for a smooth oriented surface \(\Sigma\) with boundary \(\partial\Sigma\) and a \(C^1\) vector field \(\mathbf{F}\),

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) ∮_{∂Σ} F·dr = ∬_Σ (curl F)·dS

Stokes' theorem: the line integral of F around the boundary equals the surface integral of the curl of F over the surface.

name.text: P06 Q38 - Sturm Liouville eigenvalues orthogonality

questiontext.text: The Sturm-Liouville problem \(-(py')'+qy=\lambda w y\) on \([a,b]\) (with appropriate boundary conditions) has eigenfunctions \(\phi_n\) that satisfy:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (3) ∫_a^b φₙφₘ w dx = 0 for n ≠ m (orthogonality with weight w)

The eigenfunctions of a Sturm-Liouville problem are orthogonal with respect to the weight function w: ∫_a^b φₙ(x)φₘ(x)w(x)dx = 0 for n≠m, and eigenvalues are real.

answer.text: no two eigenfunctions can be linearly independent

name.text: P06 Q39 - Central limit theorem Lindeberg

questiontext.text: The Lindeberg-Feller CLT generalizes the classical CLT to independent (not necessarily identically distributed) random variables. Its key condition is:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (2) the Lindeberg condition: no single term dominates the variance

Lindeberg condition: (1/sₙ²) Σₖ E[Xₖ²1(|Xₖ|>εsₙ)] → 0 for all ε>0, where sₙ²=ΣVar(Xₖ). This prevents any single variable from dominating.

answer.text: the variables must be identically distributed
the Lindeberg condition: \(\frac{1}{s_n^2}\sum_k E[X_k^2\mathbf{1}(|X_k|>\varepsilon s_n)]\to 0\) for all \(\varepsilon>0\)

questiontext.text: The PDE \(Au_{xx}+2Bu_{xy}+Cu_{yy}+\cdots=0\) is classified as elliptic, parabolic, or hyperbolic based on the discriminant \(\Delta=B^2-AC\). Laplace's equation \(u_{xx}+u_{yy}=0\) is classified as:

generalfeedback.text:
Answer: (1) elliptic

For Laplace's equation: A=1, B=0, C=1. Δ=0−1×1=−1<0, so it is elliptic. Elliptic PDEs model steady-state phenomena.

category.text: $course$/CSIR NET Mathematics/Model Paper 06/Part C - Advanced Mathematics

info.text: Part C: Advanced Mathematics (Q41–55). MSQ: +4, +2 partial, −1 wrong.

name.text: P06 Q41 MSQ - Measure theory sigma algebras

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about σ-algebras and measurable spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Definition of σ-algebra. (2) TRUE: Borel σ-algebra on ℝ is generated by open sets. (3) TRUE: Countable union of measurable sets is measurable. (4) TRUE: Complement of measurable set is measurable.

answer.text: A \(\sigma\)-algebra is closed under countable unions and complementation.
The Borel \(\sigma\)-algebra on \(\mathbb{R}\) is generated by all open sets.
The countable union of measurable sets is measurable.
The complement of a measurable set is measurable.

name.text: P06 Q42 MSQ - Sobolev spaces embeddings

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Sobolev spaces \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Definition — weak derivatives in Lp. (2) TRUE: Sobolev embedding theorem. (3) TRUE: Rellich-Kondrachov compact embedding. (4) FALSE: For general Ω, Sobolev spaces are NOT complete with the Lp norm alone — they require the Sobolev norm ‖u‖_{W^{k,p}}.

answer.text: \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) consists of functions whose weak derivatives up to order \(k\) lie in \(L^p(\Omega)\).
If \(kp>n\), then \(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\hookrightarrow C(\bar\Omega)\) (Sobolev embedding into continuous functions).
The embedding \(H^1(\Omega)\hookrightarrow L^2(\Omega)\) is compact for bounded \(\Omega\subset\mathbb{R}^n\) (Rellich-Kondrachov).
\(W^{k,p}(\Omega)\) with the \(L^p\) norm alone (without Sobolev norm) is always a Banach space.

name.text: P06 Q43 MSQ - Algebraic geometry basics

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about algebraic varieties (over an algebraically closed field)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: Affine varieties correspond to radical ideals (Nullstellensatz). (2) TRUE: Every affine variety is the zero set of a finite set of polynomials (Noetherian). (3) FALSE: ℙ¹ is a compact variety (projective), but not every variety is compact in the Zariski topology. (4) TRUE: Hilbert's Nullstellensatz.

answer.text: Affine varieties over \(\bar k\) are in bijection with radical ideals in \(k[x_1,\ldots,x_n]\) (Hilbert's Nullstellensatz).
Every affine variety is the zero set of finitely many polynomials (because polynomial rings are Noetherian).
Every algebraic variety is compact in the Zariski topology.
Hilbert's Nullstellensatz: \(\sqrt{I(V)}=I\) gives a dictionary between varieties and radical ideals.

name.text: P06 Q44 MSQ - Linear programming duality

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about linear programming?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Simplex method. (2) TRUE: LP duality — any feasible primal value ≤ any feasible dual value. (3) TRUE: Strong duality. (4) TRUE: Complementary slackness conditions.

answer.text: The simplex method optimizes a linear objective over a convex polytope by moving along edges.
Weak duality: the optimal value of the minimization dual is at least the optimal value of the maximization primal.
Strong duality: if both primal and dual are feasible, their optimal values are equal.
Complementary slackness: at optimality, for each constraint, either the constraint is tight or the corresponding dual variable is zero.

name.text: P06 Q45 MSQ - Galois theory splitting fields

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Galois extensions?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Galois extension = normal + separable. (2) TRUE: Fundamental theorem of Galois theory. (3) TRUE: Galois extension of degree n has Galois group of order n. (4) TRUE: Fixed field of Gal(E/F) is F for a Galois extension.

answer.text: An extension \(E/F\) is Galois if and only if it is both normal and separable.
The fundamental theorem of Galois theory gives a bijection between intermediate fields of \(E/F\) and subgroups of \(\text{Gal}(E/F)\).
The fixed field of \(\text{Gal}(E/F)\) acting on \(E\) is precisely \(F\).

name.text: P06 Q46 MSQ - Spectral theorem normal operators

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the spectral theorem for bounded operators on Hilbert spaces?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Normal operators (TT*=T*T) have spectral decomposition. (2) TRUE: Self-adjoint = real spectrum. (3) TRUE: Unitary operators have spectrum on unit circle. (4) TRUE: A normal finite-dimensional matrix is unitarily diagonalizable.

answer.text: Every normal operator (\(TT^*=T^*T\)) on a Hilbert space has a spectral decomposition.
Unitary operators have spectrum contained in the unit circle.
A normal \(n\times n\) complex matrix is unitarily diagonalizable.

name.text: P06 Q47 MSQ - Differential forms exterior algebra

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about differential forms on smooth manifolds?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Stokes theorem for forms. (2) TRUE: d∘d=0 (boundary of boundary = 0). (3) TRUE: Closed = dω=0; exact = ω=dη. (4) TRUE: De Rham cohomology.

answer.text: Stokes' theorem: \(\int_M d\omega=\int_{\partial M}\omega\) for a smooth \((n-1)\)-form on an oriented \(n\)-manifold \(M\).
\(d^2=0\): the exterior derivative applied twice gives zero.
A form \(\omega\) is closed if \(d\omega=0\); it is exact if \(\omega=d\eta\). Every exact form is closed.
De Rham cohomology \(H^k_{\text{dR}}(M)\) measures the failure of closed forms to be exact.

name.text: P06 Q48 MSQ - Distributions Dirac delta

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Dirac delta distribution \(\delta(x)\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Defining property. (2) TRUE: Derivative in distributional sense. (3) TRUE: Fourier transform of δ. (4) TRUE: Fundamental solution of −Δ in ℝⁿ (for n≥3).

answer.text: The distributional derivative of \(H(x)\) (Heaviside step) is \(\delta(x)\).
The Fourier transform of \(\delta(x)\) is the constant function 1.
\(\delta(x)\) arises naturally as the fundamental solution of the Laplace equation in \(\mathbb{R}^n\) for \(n\geq 3\).

name.text: P06 Q49 MSQ - Zeta function and prime numbers

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Riemann zeta function \(\zeta(s)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty n^{-s}\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Converges absolutely for Re(s)>1. (2) TRUE: Euler product formula. (3) TRUE: Functional equation/meromorphic continuation. (4) TRUE: Non-trivial zeros — Riemann hypothesis states they lie on Re(s)=1/2.

answer.text: \(\zeta(s)\) has a meromorphic continuation to all of \(\mathbb{C}\) with a simple pole at \(s=1\).
The Riemann hypothesis conjectures that all non-trivial zeros of \(\zeta(s)\) lie on the critical line \(\text{Re}(s)=1/2\).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the geometric distribution \(X\sim\text{Geom}(p)\) (\(P(X=k)=(1-p)^{k-1}p\), \(k=1,2,\ldots\))?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: E[X]=1/p. (2) TRUE: Geometric has the memoryless property among discrete distributions. (3) TRUE: Var(X)=(1−p)/p². (4) TRUE: P(X>n+m|X>n)=P(X>m).

answer.text: The geometric distribution is the only discrete memoryless distribution.

name.text: P06 Q51 MSQ - Brouwer fixed point

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the Brouwer fixed-point theorem?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: Statement of Brouwer. (2) TRUE: Sperner's lemma provides a combinatorial proof. (3) TRUE: Nash equilibrium existence follows from Kakutani (generalization). (4) FALSE: Brouwer applies to convex compact sets; it does NOT hold for all compact spaces (e.g., annulus has no fixed point for 180° rotation).

answer.text: Every continuous function \(f:B^n\to B^n\) from the closed \(n\)-ball to itself has a fixed point.
Brouwer's theorem can be proved using Sperner's lemma (combinatorial approach).
Nash's equilibrium existence theorem (in game theory) follows from the Kakutani fixed-point theorem, which generalizes Brouwer's.
Brouwer's theorem holds for every continuous self-map on every compact topological space.

name.text: P06 Q52 MSQ - Bounded variation functions

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about functions of bounded variation (BV) on \([a,b]\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: Jordan decomposition. (2) TRUE: BV implies L¹ (in particular Riemann integrable). (3) TRUE: BV functions are differentiable a.e. (4) TRUE: Monotone functions are BV.

answer.text: Every BV function can be written as the difference of two monotone nondecreasing functions (Jordan decomposition).
Every BV function on \([a,b]\) is bounded and hence Riemann integrable.
BV functions are differentiable almost everywhere.
Every monotone function on \([a,b]\) has bounded variation.

name.text: P06 Q53 MSQ - Numerical methods ODEs Runge-Kutta

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about the classical fourth-order Runge-Kutta (RK4) method for ODEs?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3)

(1) TRUE: RK4 uses 4 function evaluations per step. (2) TRUE: Global error is O(h⁴). (3) TRUE: RK4 is a self-starting method (no need for initial values at previous steps). (4) FALSE: RK4 is explicit — not implicit. Implicit methods (like Crank-Nicolson) allow larger stable step sizes.

answer.text: RK4 requires four function evaluations per step.
The global error of RK4 is \(O(h^4)\) for sufficiently smooth problems.
RK4 is a self-starting single-step method (requires only the current value to advance).

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about Fredholm integral equations of the second kind \(\phi(x)=f(x)+\lambda\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(4)

(1) TRUE: The integral operator is compact on L². (2) TRUE: Fredholm alternative. (3) FALSE: For λ≠0 not an eigenvalue, there IS a unique solution (Fredholm alternative). (4) TRUE: Symmetric kernels have real eigenvalues.

answer.text: The integral operator \(T\phi(x)=\int_a^b K(x,t)\phi(t)\,dt\) with square-integrable kernel is compact on \(L^2\).
The Fredholm alternative: for \(\lambda\neq 0\), either the equation has a unique solution for every \(f\), or the homogeneous equation has nontrivial solutions.
For all nonzero \(\lambda\), the Fredholm equation never has a unique solution.
If \(K\) is symmetric (\(K(x,t)=K(t,x)\)), the eigenvalues of \(T\) are real.

name.text: P06 Q55 MSQ - Probability generating functions

questiontext.text: Which of the following are TRUE about probability generating functions (PGF) \(G_X(z)=E[z^X]\) for non-negative integer-valued \(X\)?

generalfeedback.text:
Correct: (1),(2),(3),(4)

(1) TRUE: G_X(1) = E[1^X] = 1. (2) TRUE: G'_X(1) = E[X]. (3) TRUE: For independent X,Y: G_{X+Y}=G_X·G_Y. (4) TRUE: PGF of Poisson(λ) is e^{λ(z−1)}.
